words.shortDefinition: Elastic Compute Cloud; provides scalable computing capacity in the AWS cloud.
words.simpleDefinition: Amazon's service for renting virtual computers in the cloud — you can launch, configure, and run servers without buying physical hardware.
words.longDefinition: Allows users to rent virtual computers (instances) to run their own computer applications. EC2 provides resizable compute capacity and is designed to make web-scale cloud computing easier for developers.
words.techContext: We launched a fleet of t3.medium EC2 instances to handle the processing of our batch jobs.
words.exampleSentence: I SSH'd into the EC2 instance to check the logs and see why the web server had stopped responding.
words.etymology: Acronym for Elastic Compute Cloud (two Cs).
words.connector: Think of EC2 like renting an apartment instead of buying a house — you get a fully functional space you can configure, but AWS handles the building and maintenance.

words.shortDefinition: Simple Storage Service; an object storage service offering high scalability and data availability.
words.simpleDefinition: Amazon's cloud storage service for files and data — like a massive online hard drive where you can store anything and access it from anywhere.
words.longDefinition: Used to store and protect any amount of data for a range of use cases, such as websites, mobile applications, backup and restore, and big data analytics. Data is stored as 'objects' within 'buckets'.
words.techContext: All user-uploaded profile pictures are stored in an S3 bucket with restricted public access.
words.exampleSentence: We configured an S3 lifecycle policy to automatically move old logs to cheaper Glacier storage after 90 days.
words.etymology: Acronym for Simple Storage Service (three Ss).
words.connector: Think of S3 like a self-storage facility — you rent space to store your stuff, pay only for what you use, and can access it anytime without maintaining the building.

words.shortDefinition: Virtual Private Cloud; a private, isolated section of the AWS cloud.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to launch AWS resources in a virtual network that you define. You have complete control over your virtual networking environment, including selection of your own IP address range, creation of subnets, and configuration of route tables and network gateways.
words.techContext: For security, we placed our database in a private subnet within our VPC so it's not accessible from the public internet.
words.exampleSentence: I had to update the VPC security group to allow incoming traffic on port 443 for our new load balancer.

words.shortDefinition: Identity and Access Management; securely manages access to AWS services and resources.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to create and manage AWS users and groups, and use permissions to allow or deny their access to AWS resources. It follows the principle of 'least privilege'.
words.techContext: We created an IAM role for the Lambda function that only gives it permission to write to a specific DynamoDB table.
words.exampleSentence: The developer couldn't pull the images because their IAM user lacked the 'ecr:GetDownloadUrlForLayer' permission.
words.etymology: Acronym for Identity and Access Management.

words.shortDefinition: A serverless, event-driven compute service.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to run code without provisioning or managing servers. You pay only for the compute time you consume—there is no charge when your code is not running. It scales automatically based on the number of incoming requests.
words.techContext: We used an AWS Lambda function to automatically resize images whenever they are uploaded to our S3 bucket.
words.exampleSentence: The Lambda function's execution time exceeded its timeout limit because the third-party API was responding slowly.
words.etymology: Named after the 'lambda' symbol in calculus and functional programming.

words.shortDefinition: Relational Database Service; a managed service for relational databases.
words.longDefinition: Makes it easy to set up, operate, and scale a relational database in the cloud. It supports engines like PostgreSQL, MySQL, MariaDB, Oracle, and Microsoft SQL Server, handling tasks like backups, patching, and scaling.
words.techContext: By moving our MySQL database to RDS, we no longer have to manually manage OS updates or database backups.
words.exampleSentence: We enabled Multi-AZ deployment for our RDS instance to ensure high availability and automatic failover.

words.shortDefinition: A fully managed NoSQL database service.
words.longDefinition: Provides fast and predictable performance with seamless scalability. It is a key-value and document database that delivers single-digit millisecond performance at any scale.
words.techContext: We chose DynamoDB for our user session store because it can handle millions of requests per second without any manual sharding.
words.exampleSentence: The query on DynamoDB failed because we reached the provisioned read capacity units (RCU) for the table.

words.shortDefinition: A highly available and scalable cloud Domain Name System (DNS) web service.
words.longDefinition: It is designed to give developers and businesses an extremely reliable and cost-effective way to route end users to Internet applications by translating names (like www.example.com) into IP addresses.
words.techContext: We used Route 53 to set up a latency-based routing policy to send users to the AWS region closest to them.
words.exampleSentence: I registered the new domain through Route 53 and created an Alias record pointing to our CloudFront distribution.
words.etymology: Reference to Port 53 (the standard DNS port) + 'Route' (navigation).

words.shortDefinition: A fast content delivery network (CDN) service.
words.longDefinition: Securely delivers data, videos, applications, and APIs to customers globally with low latency and high transfer speeds. It integrates with other AWS services like S3 and EC2.
words.techContext: By putting CloudFront in front of our S3 bucket, we reduced image load times for our users in Europe by over 60%.
words.exampleSentence: After updating the assets, I had to create a CloudFront invalidation to ensure users see the latest version of the CSS.

words.shortDefinition: A fully managed service that makes it easy for developers to create, publish, and secure APIs at any scale.
words.longDefinition: Acts as the 'front door' for applications to access data, business logic, or functionality from your backend services. It handles tasks like traffic management, CORS support, authorization, and throttling.
words.techContext: We used API Gateway to expose our Lambda functions as a RESTful API for our mobile app.
words.exampleSentence: I configured a usage plan in API Gateway to limit the number of requests a single user can make to 10 per second.

words.shortDefinition: Simple Queue Service; a fully managed message queuing service.
words.longDefinition: Enables you to decouple and scale microservices, distributed systems, and serverless applications. SQS eliminates the complexity and overhead associated with managing and operating message-oriented middleware.
words.techContext: When a user places an order, we push a message to an SQS queue so the processing service can handle it asynchronously.
words.exampleSentence: The SQS queue acted as a buffer, allowing the backend to catch up with the surge of requests without dropping any data.

words.shortDefinition: Simple Notification Service; a fully managed pub/sub messaging service.
words.longDefinition: Provides message delivery from publishers to subscribers (also known as producers and consumers). Publishers communicate asynchronously with subscribers by sending messages to a topic.
words.techContext: We use SNS to broadcast events like 'new_user_registered' to multiple microservices simultaneously.
words.exampleSentence: I set up an SNS topic so that multiple Lambda functions can react to the same system alert.

words.shortDefinition: Elastic Block Store; provide block level storage volumes for use with EC2 instances.
words.longDefinition: EBS volumes are highly available and reliable storage volumes that can be attached to any running EC2 instance. They are typically used for file systems, databases, or any application that requires fine-grained updates and raw block-level storage.
words.techContext: We upgraded our EBS volume to an 'io2' type to provide higher IOPS for our database server.
words.exampleSentence: Even if we terminate the EC2 instance, the EBS volume remains intact so we can reattach our data to a new server.

words.shortDefinition: Elastic Kubernetes Service; a managed service that makes it easy to run Kubernetes on AWS.
words.longDefinition: EKS handles the heavy lifting of managing the Kubernetes Control Plane nodes, allowing you to focus on running your containerized applications without worrying about K8s infrastructure maintenance.
words.techContext: Moving from self-managed Kubernetes to EKS reduced our operational overhead by handling Control Plane upgrades automatically.
words.exampleSentence: We used Terraform to provision an EKS cluster and then deployed our services using standard Helm charts.

words.shortDefinition: Elastic Container Service; a highly scalable, high-performance container orchestration service.
words.longDefinition: AWS's proprietary container orchestrator. It supports Docker containers and allows you to easily run and scale containerized applications on AWS without the complexity of managing a full Kubernetes cluster.
words.techContext: For our smaller microservices, we chose ECS over Kubernetes because of its tighter integration with other AWS services like IAM and CloudWatch.
words.exampleSentence: I updated the ECS service definition to increase the desired count of tasks from 2 to 4.

words.shortDefinition: A serverless compute engine for containers.
words.longDefinition: Fargate works with both ECS and EKS. It allows you to run containers without having to manage the underlying EC2 instances. You define the CPU and memory requirements, and AWS handles the provisioning.
words.techContext: By using Fargate, we eliminated the need to patch or scale the servers in our cluster—we just focus on the container code.
words.exampleSentence: We switched our ECS tasks to run on Fargate so we wouldn't have to manage a cluster of EC2 instances.
words.etymology: Invented name, possibly implying 'far' reaching 'gate' way to compute.

words.shortDefinition: A monitoring and observability service for AWS resources.
words.longDefinition: Collects monitoring and operational data in the form of logs, metrics, and events. You can use it to set alarms, visualize data, and get an automated view of your AWS resources and applications.
words.techContext: I set up a CloudWatch Alarm to notify the team via Slack if the CPU usage on the production instances exceeds 80% for 5 minutes.
words.exampleSentence: I checked the CloudWatch logs to see the stack trace of the error that occurred in our Lambda function.

words.shortDefinition: A service that gives developers an easy way to create and manage a collection of related AWS resources.
words.longDefinition: AWS's native Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool. It allows you to use a template file (JSON or YAML) to describe all the AWS resources you want and then provisions them in an orderly and predictable fashion.
words.techContext: We use a CloudFormation stack to define our entire networking infrastructure, ensuring we can recreate it in any region.
words.exampleSentence: The CloudFormation update failed because one of the resources was still in the middle of another operation.

words.shortDefinition: Cloud Development Kit; an open-source software development framework to define cloud infrastructure in code.
words.longDefinition: Allows you to define AWS resources using familiar programming languages like TypeScript, Python, or Java. It compiles your code into CloudFormation templates.
words.techContext: Using the AWS CDK allowed us to use loops and logic to generate our infrastructure, which is much more powerful than raw YAML.
words.exampleSentence: I wrote a CDK construct to standardize how we deploy our S3 buckets with proper encryption and logging enabled.

words.shortDefinition: A fully managed in-memory data store service.
words.longDefinition: Supports Redis and Memcached. It allows you to seamlessly set up, run, and scale popular open-source compatible in-memory data stores in the cloud to improve the performance of web applications.
words.techContext: We used ElastiCache with Redis to cache our most expensive database queries, reducing our average page load time by 300ms.
words.exampleSentence: I had to increase the instance size for our ElastiCache cluster because we were hitting the memory limit for our session data.

words.shortDefinition: A fast, fully managed data warehouse that makes it simple and cost-effective to analyze all your data.
words.longDefinition: Designed for Large Scale Data sets and Database Migrations. It uses SQL and your existing Business Intelligence (BI) tools to provide fast performance on petabytes of structured and semi-structured data.
words.techContext: We migrated our historical sales data into Redshift so our data scientists can run complex analytical queries without affecting the production database.
words.exampleSentence: The Redshift cluster finished processing the weekly revenue report in just under 10 minutes.
words.etymology: Named after the 'redshift' phenomenon in astronomy (moving away), possibly implying moving away from Oracle (Big Red).

words.shortDefinition: A platform for streaming data on AWS.
words.longDefinition: Offers powerful services to make it easy to load and analyze streaming data, and also enables you to build custom streaming data applications for specialized needs. Features include Kinesis Data Streams and Firehose.
words.techContext: We use Kinesis Data Firehose to stream all of our application logs directly into an S3 bucket for long-term storage.
words.exampleSentence: The Kinesis stream handles the real-time ingestion of millions of IoT sensor events every hour.

words.shortDefinition: Helps you protect secrets needed to access your applications, services, and IT resources.
words.longDefinition: Enables you to easily rotate, manage, and retrieve database credentials, API keys, and other secrets throughout their lifecycle. It also provides automatic rotation for RDS databases.
words.techContext: Instead of storing the database password in a config file, our application retrieves it at runtime from AWS Secrets Manager.
words.exampleSentence: I updated the API key in Secrets Manager, and all our Lambda functions started using the new key immediately without any redeployment.

words.shortDefinition: Key Management Service; makes it easy to create and manage cryptographic keys.
words.longDefinition: Used to control the encryption of your data across a wide range of AWS services and in your applications. KMS is integrated with CloudTrail to provide logs of all key usage for regulatory and compliance needs.
words.techContext: We use a KMS customer master key (CMK) to encrypt all our sensitive data at rest in S3 and RDS.
words.exampleSentence: The application failed to decrypt the data because its IAM role didn't have the 'kms:Decrypt' permission for the specific key.

words.shortDefinition: Contains a collection of EC2 instances that are treated as a logical grouping.
words.longDefinition: Automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances in your group according to conditions you define. This ensures that you have the right number of instances to handle the load for your application while minimizing costs.
words.techContext: Our Autoscaling Group added three new instances in response to the increased latency detected by the load balancer.
words.exampleSentence: We set a minimum size of 2 for our Autoscaling Group to ensure our application remains highly available even during low traffic periods.
